title: Strings
description: C strings are null-terminated char arrays — master strlen, strcmp, strcpy, and manual character manipulation for interview string problems.

sections.title: Char arrays as strings
sections.explanation: In C, strings are just `char` arrays terminated by a null character `'\0'`. Declare with `char s[] = "hello"` (compiler adds the null). You can also declare with a fixed size: `char s[100] = "hello"`. Access individual characters with `s[i]`.

exercise.prompt: Create a string "world". Print it, print its first and last characters, then change the first character to 'W' and print the modified string. Finally print its length.

validation.message: Print the string, first char 'w', last char 'd', modified 'World', length 5
Use s[0] for first, s[4] for last, and strlen for length

exercise.hint: First char is s[0] = 'w', last is s[4] = 'd'. strlen("world") returns 5.

sections.title: strcmp and strcpy
sections.explanation: **strcmp(a, b)** compares two strings: returns 0 if equal, negative if a < b, positive if a > b. **strcpy(dest, src)** copies src into dest (dest must be large enough). **strcat(dest, src)** appends src to dest. These are in `<string.h>`.

exercise.prompt: Compare strings "cat" and "cat" using strcmp and print "equal" if they match. Then copy "hello" into a buffer and concatenate " world" onto it. Print the result.

validation.message: strcmp returns 0 for equal strings, then concat gives 'hello world'
Use strcmp == 0, strcpy to copy, strcat to concatenate

exercise.hint: strcmp returns 0 when strings are equal. strcpy copies, strcat appends.

sections.title: Iterating and manipulating characters
sections.explanation: Loop through a string with `for (int i = 0; s[i] != '\0'; i++)` or use `strlen`. Character math works because chars are integers: `c - 'a'` gives 0-25 index, `c - '0'` converts digit char to int. Use `tolower()` and `toupper()` from `<ctype.h>` for case conversion.

exercise.prompt: Count the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u — lowercase only) in the string "interview". Print the count.

validation.message: "interview" has 4 vowels: i, e, i, e
Iterate until null terminator and increment count for each vowel

exercise.hint: "interview" has vowels: i, e, i, e — that's 4. Wait: i-n-t-e-r-v-i-e-w. The vowels are i, e, i, e = 4.

sections.title: Reversing a string in-place
sections.explanation: A classic interview task: reverse a C string using two pointers. Swap characters from both ends moving inward. This is O(n) time, O(1) space — the optimal approach. You'll reuse this swap pattern in many problems.

exercise.prompt: Reverse the string "interview" in-place using the two-pointer swap technique. Print the reversed string.

validation.message: Use two-pointer swap with a temp variable

exercise.hint: Swap s[left] and s[right] using temp. s[left] = s[right]; s[right] = temp;
